title: Corrected Hawking Temperature and Final State of Black Hole Evaporation Under GEVAG Framework
summary: In the GEVAG (Generalized Entropy Varying-G) framework, any generalization to horizon entropy leads to a varying gravitational "constant" $G_\text{eff}$ that is a function the horizon area. In this work, it is shown that if we promote $G_\text{eff}$ to be valid in the neighborhood of the horizon, then Hawking temperature consists of two terms, the second of which is related to the variation of $G_\text{eff}$. When applied to the logarithmic correction of the entropy, as is common across various quantum gravity approaches, the first term in the Schwarzschild black hole temperature exactly agrees with that obtained from utilizing generalized uncertainty principle (GUP), while the second term improves on the GUP result by driving the Hawking temperature to zero as the black hole approaches a minimum mass. This resolves the inconsistency in the GUP result concerning a nonzero temperature minimum mass remnant. This work also derives simple general formulas for both the thermodynamic energy and the Bekenstein bound for any correction to the area law under the same assumption that $G_\text{eff}$ can be extended off-shell in the horizon neighborhood. The (generalized) Bekenstein bound can be interpreted as a statement regarding the renormalization group scaling dimension of the entropy functional $f(A)$ and the naturalness of the theory.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the GEVAG framework, showing that a varying effective gravitational constant (G_eff) near the horizon modifies Hawking temperature, resolving inconsistencies in GUP results and establishing generalized thermodynamic relations.
AI.motivation: To address the inconsistency in generalized uncertainty principle (GUP) results regarding nonzero temperature at minimum mass remnants and to derive thermodynamic relations under a varying G_eff assumption.
AI.method: Promotes G_eff (dependent on horizon area) to be valid near the horizon, leading to a two-term Hawking temperature. Applies this to logarithmic entropy corrections common in quantum gravity theories.
AI.result: First term matches GUP-derived temperature for Schwarzschild black holes; second term drives temperature to zero at minimum mass, resolving the GUP inconsistency. Derives thermodynamic energy and generalized Bekenstein bounds linked to renormalization group scaling.
AI.conclusion: The GEVAG approach provides a consistent thermodynamic framework correcting GUP limitations, with implications for entropy's renormalization group scaling and theory naturalness.

title: The unusually red delay spectrum of the low-mass black hole AGN NGC\,4051 as revealed by intensive continuum reverberation mapping with the Las Cumbres Observatory
comment: 24 pages, 18 figures. Accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: We present a two-year optical reverberation mapping campaign of NGC 4051, an active galactic nucleus (AGN) hosting a low-mass black hole ($8\times10^5 M_\odot$), using daily observations in seven photometric bands from Las Cumbres Observatory augmented by archival data from Swift XRT and UVOT. The light curves show correlated variability with wavelength-dependent lags broadly consistent with the standard accretion disc scaling, $τ\propto λ^{4/3}$, and a pronounced u-band excess. However, the $i$ and $z_s$ lags are significantly larger than expected and cannot be explained by a combination of disc emission and diffuse continuum (DC) from the broad-line region (BLR), making NGC 4051 a notable lag-luminosity outlier. The spectral energy distribution (SED) of the variable AGN component is markedly redder than the canonical accretion disc prediction, $F_ν\propto ν^{1/3}$, typically observed in more massive systems. We explore two scenarios to account for the red UV-optical SED and the anomalously large $i$ and $z_s$ lags: (a) SMC-like dust reddening ($E(B-V)\sim0.18$) combined with optically thick emission from the inner edge of the dusty torus; and (b) a dominant diffuse continuum contribution. We discuss the implications of each scenario within a comprehensive multi-wavelength framework.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a two-year optical reverberation mapping campaign of NGC 4051, an AGN with a low-mass black hole, revealing unexpected lags in the i and z_s bands and a redder-than-expected SED. Two scenarios are explored to explain these anomalies.
AI.motivation: To investigate the unique properties of NGC 4051's variability, particularly its red UV-optical SED and large i/z_s lags, which challenge standard accretion disk models and require alternative explanations.
AI.method: Conducted daily multi-band photometric observations using Las Cumbres Observatory, supplemented with Swift XRT/UVOT data. Analyzed light curves for wavelength-dependent lags and spectral energy distribution, testing models involving dust reddening and diffuse continuum contributions.
AI.result: Observed lags in i and z_s bands exceed disc+diffuse continuum predictions, and the SED is significantly redder than the ν^{1/3} expectation. Results exclude simple disc models; dust reddening with a dusty torus or dominant diffuse continuum are viable candidates.
AI.conclusion: NGC 4051's anomalies suggest deviations from standard AGN accretion models. Future observations and modeling are needed to confirm dust reddening from the torus or a dominant diffuse continuum as the primary explanation.

title: Laser-assisted production of the light charged Higgs boson from top quark decay in the type-I two Higgs doublet model
summary: We investigate the impact of a circularly polarized laser field on the top quark decay process into a charged Higgs boson ($t\rightarrow bH^+$) within the type-I two Higgs doublet model. Our study aims to explore how an external electromagnetic field can modify key observables and potentially facilitate the experimental detection of the charged Higgs boson, addressing challenges related to missing energy in collider experiments such as the LHC. Employing the Dirac-Volkov formalism, we model the interaction between charged particles and the laser field and demonstrate that the presence of the laser can notably influence the decay branching ratios under suitable conditions. The analysis reveals that both the intensity and frequency of the laser field play a crucial role in determining the decay width. In particular, for a laser field strength of $3.8\times 10^{14}$ V/cm and a photon energy of $0.117$ eV, the branching ratio of the top quark decaying into a charged Higgs boson with mass in the range $80$-$150$ GeV and a bottom quark reaches $0.97$, surpassing the standard $t\rightarrow bW^+$ channel. These results suggest that strong electromagnetic fields can serve as an effective mechanism to enhance signals of new particles, offering promising avenues for experimental searches beyond the Standard Model.

AI.tldr: The study examines how a circularly polarized laser field affects the top quark decay into a charged Higgs boson (t→bH+) in a two Higgs doublet model. Under specific laser conditions, the branching ratio for this decay reaches 0.97, overtaking the standard t→bW+ channel, highlighting the potential of strong electromagnetic fields in detecting new particles.
AI.motivation: To explore how external laser fields can modify observables and improve detection chances of charged Higgs bosons, addressing challenges like missing energy in LHC experiments.
AI.method: Using the Dirac-Volkov formalism to model particle-laser interactions, the analysis calculates decay widths under varying laser intensities and frequencies.
AI.result: Laser parameters (3.8e14 V/cm strength and 0.117 eV photon energy) significantly boost the branching ratio for t→bH+ to 0.97 for charged Higgs masses 80-150 GeV, surpassing the standard W+ channel.
AI.conclusion: Strong electromagnetic fields can enhance signals of new particles, providing new experimental opportunities beyond the Standard Model.

title: CORSIKA 8: A General Framework for Particle Cascade Simulations
summary: The simulation of extensive air showers and particle cascades in general is a cornerstone of modern astroparticle physics. For more than two decades, CORSIKA, currently in version 7, has been one of the most widely used tools for this purpose. However, its architecture reflects design constraints of an earlier computing era, as well as increasingly limiting extensibility, maintainability, and adaptability to modern experimental requirements. CORSIKA 8 is a complete redesign of the original CORSIKA code, implemented in modern C++ and based on contemporary software engineering principles. It introduces a modular and extensible simulation framework with explicit handling of units, flexible geometry, and environment descriptions. In this paper, we present the design philosophy and core architecture of CORSIKA 8, describe the implementation of electromagnetic and hadronic shower physics, and validate air shower simulations against CORSIKA 7. The results demonstrate good agreement at the few-percent level for key observables, confirming the physics fidelity of CORSIKA 8. We also showcase new use cases that were beyond the capabilities of version 7, such as the simulation of cross-media showers and particle cascades in ice, including radio-signal propagation

AI.tldr: CORSIKA 8 is a redesigned version of the widely used CORSIKA simulation tool, addressing previous limitations with modern C++ and modular architecture, enabling advanced simulations like cross-media showers and radio signals in ice, while maintaining compatibility with CORSIKA 7 results.
AI.motivation: To overcome the extensibility, maintainability, and adaptability limitations of CORSIKA 7 due to outdated architecture, and to meet modern experimental requirements such as simulating new environments (e.g., ice) and multi-medium scenarios.
AI.method: CORSIKA 8 employs a modular framework with modern C++, explicit unit handling, flexible geometry, and environment descriptions. It implements electromagnetic and hadronic shower physics, validated against CORSIKA 7 simulations.
AI.result: Key observables show agreement within a few percent between CORSIKA 8 and 7, confirming physics accuracy. New capabilities like simulating particle cascades in ice and radio-signal propagation demonstrate enhanced functionality.
AI.conclusion: CORSIKA 8 successfully modernizes the simulation tool, ensuring compatibility while expanding capabilities for contemporary astroparticle physics research needs.

title: Asymptotic analysis of the "simulated horizon" segment of the Collins spiral
summary: The Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff (TOV) equations for a massless fluid take the form of a pair of coupled autonomous first order differential equations, which can be employed in a model for a ``dynamical gravastar'' black hole mimicker. The mimicker has no true horizon, but rather a ``simulated horizon'', outside which the geometry resembles a Schwarzschild black hole, but inside which the $g_{00}$ component of the metric is always positive and becomes exponentially small. Collins has reinterpreted the relevant TOV equations in terms of a two-dimensional flow with a spiral form, and Zöllner and Kämpfer have mapped the simulated horizon to a specific segment of the Collins spiral. We give here results of an asymptotic analysis, relating initial values at the small radius end of this spiral segment to the black hole mimicker mass and other parameters that emerge at the large radius kink in the TOV solution, which corresponds to the simulated horizon.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff (TOV) equations for a massless fluid in modeling a dynamical gravastar as a black hole mimicker, focusing on the relationship between initial conditions and emergent parameters at the simulated horizon.
AI.motivation: To understand the connection between initial values near the core of a gravastar and properties like mass at the simulated horizon, which mimics a Schwarzschild black hole externally but avoids an actual event horizon.
AI.method: Asymptotic analysis of the TOV equations mapped onto a Collins spiral, linking initial conditions at the spiral's start to parameters at the large-radius kink representing the simulated horizon.
AI.result: Established a framework connecting microscopic initial fluid conditions to macroscopic spacetime parameters of the black hole mimicker, providing insights into gravastar dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully relates internal gravastar configurations to their horizon-like properties, offering new methods for studying black hole alternatives and their spacetime behavior without event horizons.

title: Low-mass failed supernovae and the $10\,M_{\odot}$ peak in the merging black hole mass distribution
summary: Gravitational-wave observations reveal that the rate of merging black holes drops by $\sim2$ orders of magnitude from component masses $\sim 10\,M_{\odot}$ to $\sim 15\,M_{\odot}$. The increased compactness of the black hole progenitor cores may contribute to the $\sim 10\,M_{\odot}$ overdensity, but cannot fully explain the rate difference. In this paper, we consider the possibility that the overdensity is reinforced by supernova processes that result in efficient black hole formation from direct collapse in a narrow range around $10\, M_{\odot}$. We extend previous studies by considering a distinct subpopulation of failed-supernovae black holes, possibly separated by a gap in the primary mass distribution from the rest of the population. Using 153 observations from the latest GWTC-4.0 catalog, we confirm a strong peak in the primary mass distribution at $10\,M_{\odot}$, with a peak rate density of $7.36_{-3.11}^{+6.35}$ $M_{\odot}^{-1}\mathrm{yr}^{-1} \mathrm{Gpc}^{-3}$. The rate drops sharply and becomes consistent with zero at the 90 % level for primary mass $m_1\in (12.0, 16.1)\, M_{\odot}$, then rises again to confidently nonzero values above $\sim 16\,M_{\odot}$ before falling at higher masses. Our results reveal structure in the mass distribution in the $10-20\,M_{\odot}$ range, with rate changes of multiple orders of magnitude across a few solar masses, consistent with a distinct population of failed-supernova black holes.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a significant overdensity of black holes at around 10 solar masses using gravitational-wave data, suggesting a population of failed-supernova black holes formed via direct collapse.
AI.motivation: The observed drop in merging black hole rates from 10 to 15 solar masses isn't fully explained, so the authors investigate whether failed supernova events could create a distinct population of black holes at 10 solar masses.
AI.method: Analyzed GWTC-4.0 catalog (153 observations) to study primary mass distribution, identified a sharp peak at 10 Msun with a significant drop between 12-16 Msun, then recovery above 16 Msun.
AI.result: Confirmed a strong 10 Msun peak with rate density 7.36 $M_{☉} yr^{-1} ∅^{-3}$, followed by a mass gap and subsequent increase, indicating distinct formation channels.
AI.conclusion: The mass distribution structure supports the existence of a failed-supernova black hole subpopulation forming through direct collapse, explaining the observed rate variations in LIGO/Virgo observations.

title: Origin of the Covariant Wigner Operator as a Quantum Amplitude in QCD
summary: The Wigner function plays a central role in QCD as a phase space object encoding correlations among quarks, antiquarks, and gluons, yet its interpretation remains subtle due to its quasiprobabilistic nature and possible negativity. Recent work based on the Koopman-von Neumann-Sudarshan (KvNS) Hilbert space formulation of classical mechanics suggests the Wigner function arises as a \textit{quantum probability amplitude} projected onto classical phase space, rather than a quasiprobability density \citep{wignerphasespace, wave_operator}. In the classical limit, this amplitude reduces to the classical Koopman wavefunction. In this work, we extend this perspective to relativistic QCD by constructing a Koopman description of the quark Wigner operator. We show that the Wigner operator is naturally isomorphic to a phase space spinor via an idempotent projection, providing a unified framework in which both classical and quantum dynamics are expressed. Within this formulation, the Wigner function retains its interpretation as an amplitude even in the relativistic regime. This viewpoint clarifies the origin of negativity and other nonclassical features, and provides a more transparent foundation for parton distribution functions in QCD. Remarkably, the relativistic Koopman framework reproduces the classical limit of QCD.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the Koopman-von Neumann-Sudarshan (KvNS) framework to relativistic QCD, interpreting the Wigner function as a quantum probability amplitude in phase space. It establishes an isomorphism between the Wigner operator and a phase space spinor, explaining negativity and unifying classical/quantum dynamics.
AI.motivation: To clarify the interpretation of the Wigner function in QCD, address its quasiprobabilistic nature and negativity, and provide a unified framework for classical and quantum dynamics in relativistic settings.
AI.method: Constructs a relativistic Koopman description of the quark Wigner operator, demonstrating its isomorphism to a phase space spinor via idempotent projection. Derives the classical limit within this formulation.
AI.result: Shows the Wigner function acts as a quantum amplitude in phase space even relativistically, explains negativity through this amplitude view, and confirms reproduction of the classical QCD limit.
AI.conclusion: Advances a foundational understanding of QCD's Wigner function, offering a clearer interpretation that bridges classical and quantum regimes, and supports more transparent analysis of parton distributions.

title: Geant4-IcyMoons: Simulating Electron Interaction Physics in Irradiated Astrophysical Ices
summary: Energetic particles continuously process water ice across astrophysical and planetary environments, from interstellar clouds and comets to icy planetary surfaces. Interpreting the resulting observables requires a physically grounded description of the underlying interactions, both to identify radiation-driven signatures and to distinguish them from superimposed chemical, thermal, and microphysical effects. We present Geant4-IcyMoons, an extension of Geant4-DNA developed for irradiated water ice and, ultimately, for materials embedded within it. In this study, we model the elastic and inelastic interactions of electrons with amorphous and hexagonal ice. For the first time, this enables a transport-ready Monte Carlo simulation of electron irradiation in water ice, linking incident-particle environments to the evolution of icy surfaces. We apply this framework to Jupiter's moon Europa as a representative case of electron bombardment of an icy surface. We show that, on the trailing hemisphere, the stronger low-energy electron bombardment confines much of the deposited energy to the upper $\lesssim 0.1$~cm, whereas on the leading hemisphere, the more energetic incident population drives deposition patterns to depths of tens of centimeters. This may contribute to the observed lens-like enrichment of radiolysis products centered on the equator of the trailing hemisphere. This work lays the foundation for treatments of ion irradiation and radiation chemistry in water ice and embedded materials.

AI.tldr: Geant4-IcyMoons is a new simulation tool modeling electron interactions with water ice, particularly applied to Europa's hemispheres, showing how electron energy affects ice surface evolution and radiolysis product distribution.
AI.motivation: To accurately simulate how energetic particles process water ice in space environments, distinguishing radiation effects from other factors, and understand surface evolution on icy moons.
AI.method: Extended Geant4-DNA into Geant4-IcyMoons to model elastic/inelastic electron-ice interactions, enabling Monte Carlo simulations of electron irradiation in amorphous/hexagonal ice.
AI.result: Simulations show low-energy electrons on Europa's trailing hemisphere limit energy deposition to top 0.1 cm, while leading hemisphere's high-energy electrons penetrate to 10s cm, explaining observed radiolysis patterns.
AI.conclusion: This framework advances understanding of radiation-driven ice evolution, providing a basis for future studies on ion irradiation and radiation chemistry in icy environments.

title: High-frequency gravitational wave transients from superradiance
summary: Ultralight bosons can form macroscopic gravitational-atom clouds around rotating black holes via superradiance, sourcing quasi-monochromatic gravitational waves through level transitions and annihilation. Primordial black holes provide a natural setting for such systems in a frequency range relevant for resonant-cavity experiments. We present a unified treatment of gravitational-wave emission from both isolated and binary-perturbed gravitational atoms in this regime. For isolated systems, we derive analytic expressions for the time- and frequency-domain strain from transition and annihilation channels, emphasizing their narrow-band structure. For binaries, we model resonantly driven level transitions using the Landau--Zener formalism and compute the resulting transient signals. We find that, while binary-driven transitions generically yield signals with durations compatible with detector response times, their characteristic strain lies well below the sensitivity of current experiments at astrophysically plausible distances, and event rates further suppress detectability by requiring sources at unrealistically small separations. We quantify the improvements in sensitivity, bandwidth, and response needed to render these signals observable, and identify gravitational-atom systems around primordial black holes as a theoretically well-motivated target for future high-frequency gravitational-wave searches.

AI.tldr: The paper explores gravitational-wave emission from ultralight boson clouds around primordial black holes in isolated and binary systems. While analytical models are developed for both, binary-driven signals are found to be undetectable with current technology, highlighting requirements for future detectors.
AI.motivation: To understand gravitational-wave signals from gravitational atoms (ultralight boson clouds around black holes) and assess detectability using current and future experiments.
AI.method: Derived analytical expressions for strain in isolated systems and used Landau-Zener formalism for binary-perturbed systems to model transitions and transient signals. Quantified detection challenges and required experimental improvements.
AI.result: Binary-driven signals currently undetectable due to low strain and low event rates. Future detectors need higher sensitivity, wider bandwidth, and faster response times to observe these signals.
AI.conclusion: Gravitational-atom systems around primordial black holes are promising for future high-frequency gravitational-wave searches if technological advancements are made.

title: Insights from GRBs for optical follow-up of gravitational wave counterparts
summary: Identifying the electromagnetic counterparts to gravitational wave sources is vital to enabling the myriad of investigations possible with multimessenger astronomy. However, locating faint, fast-varying transients within large localisations remains challenging given the uncertainty in their detailed properties. In this work, we investigate how the nearby merger-induced GRBs would be localised by the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA detector network during the fifth gravitational wave observing run (O5) and assess whether their optical counterparts could be detected using gravitational wave localisations alone, without additional localisation from gamma-ray instruments. Counterpart detectability is evaluated using the observed optical afterglow lightcurves of these GRBs and the distance-scaled lightcurve of the kilonova AT2017gfo as a fiducial template. We find that such events can be localised to comparatively small regions of the sky, often only a few to tens of square degrees. As a result, counterparts are detectable by at least one of the available optical telescopes during O5. However, detectability depends strongly on observational depth, as the counterparts are fainter than $22$ mag within a day. Facilities capable of reaching depths of $\gtrsim23$ mag therefore play a key role in recovering these faint counterparts. These results indicate that for such events during O5, the primary challenge for multimessenger discovery will be in achieving sufficient observational depth and reliably identifying the true counterpart among unrelated transients rather than gravitational wave localisation itself.

AI.tldr: The paper examines whether electromagnetic counterparts of merger-induced GRBs can be localized and detected via gravitational wave signals alone during O5, finding that while localization is achievable within small sky regions, detecting faint optical counterparts depends on telescopes reaching ~23 mag depth to distinguish them from other transients.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to assess the feasibility of detecting GRB optical counterparts using only gravitational wave localizations during O5, addressing the challenge of locating faint, fast transients in large areas without gamma-ray data.
AI.method: The study uses LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA's expected O5 gravitational wave localizations combined with observed optical afterglow lightcurves of GRBs and the AT2017gfo kilonova template scaled by distance to predict detectability by optical telescopes.
AI.result: Events can be localized to  few-to-tens-of-square-degree regions, but optical counterparts (fainter than 22 mag within a day) require telescopes reaching ≥23 mag depth for detection, with identification challenges among unrelated transients.
AI.conclusion: The key challenges for multimessenger discovery during O5 are achieving sufficient observational depth and reliably identifying true counterparts, rather than gravitational wave localization itself.

title: Mechanical Equilibrium in the Magnetized Quark--Hadron Mixed Phase: A Covariant Generalization of the Gibbs Condition
summary: We formulate a covariant mechanical equilibrium condition for the quark-hadron mixed phase boundary in the presence of a magnetic-field-induced pressure anisotropy. Using the \emph{relativistic thin-shell} formalism to describe the quark-hadron boundary, we interpret conservation of stress-energy across the interface as a set of generalized Young--Laplace conditions which characterize the geometry of the interface. In a comoving stationary frame, this provides a covariant description of mechanical equilibrium at the interface, which serves as a replacement for the scalar pressure-balance condition used in the isotropic Gibbs construction.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes a covariant equilibrium condition for the quark-hadron mixed phase under magnetic-field-induced pressure anisotropy, using a relativistic thin-shell formalism to generalize Young-Laplace conditions for anisotropic mechanical balance.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of the traditional Gibbs construction in handling magnetic-induced pressure anisotropy by developing a covariant framework that properly accounts for geometric and relativistic effects at phase boundaries.
AI.method: Applied the relativistic thin-shell formalism to derive generalized Young-Laplace equations, incorporating stress-energy conservation across the quark-hadron interface in a comoving frame.
AI.result: Demonstrated a covariant mechanical equilibrium description replacing scalar pressure-balance, showing how magnetic fields affect phase boundary geometry through anisotropic stress components.
AI.conclusion: The covariant approach provides a necessary framework for studying magnetized compact objects and phase transitions with anisotropic pressures, advancing beyond classical Gibbs equilibrium conditions.

title: A research roadmap for assessing the feasibility of warming Mars
summary: This roadmap outlines research pathways to determine whether Mars could be warmed with non-biological methods. It does not presuppose that warming Mars is desirable; its purpose is to identify what would need to be true for Mars to be warmed, what it would cost, and what could go wrong. Three complementary research tracks appear promising. Solid-state greenhouse membranes offer local warming, aiding water harvesting, food production, and oxygen supply near human bases. Orbiting reflectors can warm key sites such as bases and CO$_2$-ice reservoirs, although a large combined area would be required. Strengthening Mars' natural greenhouse effect might warm large regions or the globe, although many aspects remain to be worked out. Each approach carries scientific and technical risks that research must address. Near-term priorities are on-Earth testing of key parameters that will determine whether engineered aerosol warming is realistically possible, assessing whether exponential production of bioplastic habitats is possible, and designing at-Mars process experiments. In the near term, the research proposed here is closely aligned with and supports research needed to understand Mars' atmosphere and volatile evolution and hazards to human explorers. The main external uncertainty is whether or not launch costs continue to fall. This is early-stage research, and we discuss key near-term decision points, alternative pathways, and payoffs if research outcomes are negative. We also outline build-out pathways if research succeeds and demand exists. Relatively modest research investments would keep open the option of extending life beyond Earth as Mars' scientific exploration continues.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the feasibility of warming Mars using non-biological methods through three research tracks: solid-state greenhouse membranes, orbital reflectors, and enhancing Mars' natural greenhouse effect. It emphasizes near-term Earth-based experiments and addresses risks and uncertainties, arguing for continued research investment to maintain the possibility of terraforming Mars.
AI.motivation: To determine the practicality, costs, and risks of warming Mars non-biologically, without assuming its desirability. The goal is to identify necessary conditions, potential failures, and required research pathways.
AI.method: Proposes three complementary research directions: (1) solid-state membranes for localized warming, (2) orbital reflectors to target key areas, and (3) enhancing greenhouse effects for large-scale warming. Prioritizes Earth-based testing of aerosol techniques, bioplastic habitat scalability, and in-situ Mars experiments.
AI.result: Highlights near-term research alignment with Mars atmospheric science and exploration safety. Notes launch cost trends as critical uncertainty. Outlines decision points, alternative paths, and scenarios for successful or failed research outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Argues that modest investments in early-stage research are essential to keep Mars terraforming options viable, contingent on continued scientific exploration and technological breakthroughs.

title: Probing Black Hole Thermodynamics and Microstructure via the Shadow of Sagittarius A*
summary: We explore the connection between black hole shadows, thermodynamic phase structure, and microstructure of charged and rotating black holes within General Relativity and Geometrothermodynamics. Focusing on Reissner-Nordström and Kerr solutions, we establish a criterion to select the most suitable Geometrothermodynamic metric for a system, revealing that the first metric from enthalpy and the second from mass correctly reproduce heat capacity singularities. We show that the shadow radius encodes the same phase information as entropy and introduce Shadow-Microstructure diagrams to extract insights into stability and microscopic interaction types directly from observational bounds. Applying this framework to Sagittarius A*, we constrain the macroscopic parameters and the allowed microscopic thermodynamic phases. Our findings indicate that shadow measurements offer a novel probe of thermodynamic and microscopic aspects of black holes, enabling tests of alternative theories of gravity and thermodynamic frameworks.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes a connection between black hole shadows, thermodynamics, and microstructure using GR and Geometrothermodynamics, providing a novel method to probe black hole properties through shadow observations.
AI.motivation: To explore how black hole shadows can reveal thermodynamic phase structures and microscale interactions, enabling tests of gravity theories and thermodynamic models.
AI.method: Analyzes Reissner-Nordström and Kerr black holes using Geometrothermodynamic metrics, identifying suitable metrics based on heat capacity singularities. Introduces Shadow-Microstructure diagrams to link shadow data with phase stability and microscopic interactions.
AI.result: Demonstrates that shadow radius encodes phase information like entropy; applies to Sagittarius A* to constrain parameters and allowed thermodynamic phases. Shows shadow measurements are a new probe for black hole thermodynamics.
AI.conclusion: Shadow observations offer a unique way to study black hole thermodynamics and microscopic physics, potentially validating or challenging existing theories of gravity and thermodynamics.

title: Impacts of Voids, Line of Sight Interactions, and Local Emission Environment on Detectability of Gamma-Ray AGN
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures. Accepted for publication in ApJ
summary:
Cosmic voids may have novel affects on the propagation of high-energy photons. We consider the fraction of the line of sight that intersect voids (termed \enquote{voidiness}). A previous study showed that active galactic nuclei (AGN) detected by \textit{Fermi} Large Area Telescope (LAT) lie along voidier lines of sight than redshift-matched populations of Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) optically detected quasars in the redshift range from $0.4 \leq z < 0.7$.
  We explore this difference and various astrophysical explanations for it. Weaker intergalactic magnetic fields in voids would naturally enhance the gamma-ray cascading flux within the \textit{Fermi}-LAT point-spread function. We find that line-of-sight interactions increasing the flux in the \textit{Fermi}-LAT energy band by $\sim$0.1\% per Mpc of void traversed may be sufficient to result in the observed difference in voidiness distributions. Voidiness comparisons between SDSS QSO and AGN detected by imaging atmospheric Cherenkov telescopes at very-high-energies (VHE) do not yield any conclusive statement, likely because of the limited VHE sample size, and therefore are inconclusive about the role of possibly weaker extragalactic background light within voids. Finally, we measure that $28 \pm 3 \%$ of gamma-ray detected sources exist within a void (consistent with random mock populations) compared to $19.1 \pm 0.3 \%$ of SDSS quasars. We do not find any significant local void effect for gamma-ray sources that would explain the voidiness difference between \textit{Fermi}-LAT gamma-ray and SDSS QSO sources. These results suggest that the observed difference in voidiness distributions may be due to line-of-sight interactions rather than the local emission environment of gamma-ray AGN.

AI.tldr: The study investigates why gamma-ray detected AGN from Fermi-LAT appear along voidier lines of sight than SDSS quasars at redshifts 0.4–0.7. It finds that weaker intergalactic magnetic fields in voids could enhance gamma-ray cascading, explaining the observed difference through line-of-sight interactions rather than local void environments.
AI.motivation: To understand the discrepancy in voidiness distributions between Fermi-LAT AGN and SDSS quasars, and explore astrophysical mechanisms causing this difference.
AI.method: Analyzed voidiness (fraction of line of sight through voids) for Fermi-LAT AGN vs SDSS QSO samples. Simulated effects of weaker intergalactic magnetic fields in voids on gamma-ray cascading flux. Compared results with observational data and mock populations.
AI.result: A ~0.1% flux increase per Mpc of void traverse via magnetic field effects could explain the voidiness difference. No significant local void emission effects found. Limited VHE data prevent conclusive EBL role assessment. 28±3% Fermi sources vs 19.1±0.3% SDSS quasars lie within voids.
AI.conclusion: The voidiness difference likely arises from line-of-sight interactions (e.g., magnetic field effects) rather than local environments of AGN. Further VHE observations needed to confirm intergalactic magnetic field and EBL hypotheses.

title: Quantum Fisher Information as a Probe of Sterile Neutrino New Physics:Geometric Advantage of KM3NeT over IceCube
summary: We investigate a reported discrepancy between a high-energy neutrino detection at KM3NeT and its non-observation at IceCube, which suggests a statistical tension of up to 3.5 standard deviations. This gap has been proposed to arise from sterile neutrino oscillations over the 147-kilometer path to KM3NeT, driven by either matter-induced resonances or nonstandard interactions. Using the Quantum Fisher Information framework, we quantify the sensitivity of the neutrino state to these new physics couplings and establish fundamental precision limits via the quantum Cramer-Rao bound. Our analysis shows that the information available at KM3NeT exceeds that at IceCube by three orders of magnitude for matter-induced scenarios. We demonstrate that IceCube would require over thirty times more events to match the precision of a single KM3NeT detection. We identify an optimal baseline of 150 to 200 kilometers, placing KM3NeT in a superior position for these measurements. Our results show that standard detection methods already reach the ultimate quantum precision limit, and that a small number of future events at KM3NeT could provide the first quantum-limited constraints on sterile neutrino couplings.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the discrepancy between high-energy neutrino detections at KM3NeT and IceCube, attributing it to sterile neutrino oscillations. Using quantum Fisher information, it shows KM3NeT's superior sensitivity due to its baseline, requiring fewer events to match IceCube's precision. Future KM3NeT data could yield quantum-limited constraints on sterile neutrinos.
AI.motivation: To resolve the statistical tension (3.5σ) between KM3NeT and IceCube neutrino observations, which hints at new physics like sterile neutrinos. The study aims to quantify detector sensitivity and evaluate measurement precision limits.
AI.method: Applies the Quantum Fisher Information framework to assess sensitivity to sterile neutrino couplings (matter-induced resonances or nonstandard interactions). Compares information content between KM3NeT (147 km baseline) and IceCube, calculates event requirements, and identifies optimal baseline ranges (150-200 km).
AI.result: KM3NeT has three orders of magnitude more information than IceCube for matter-induced scenarios. IceCube needs 30x more events to match KM3NeT's precision. Optimal baselines confirm KM3NeT's优势. Current methods already achieve quantum limits, enabling future events to impose strict sterile neutrino constraints.
AI.conclusion: KM3NeT's unique position due to baseline and sensitivity allows it to outperform IceCube. Future low-event datasets could provide first quantum-limited sterile neutrino constraints, emphasizing KM3NeT's critical role in new physics searches.

title: Geometric origin of the cosmological constant from Einstein-Chern-Simons gravity compactified to four dimensions
summary:
We present a model in which the cosmological constant emerges as a purely geometric effect from the four-dimensional compactification of five-dimensional Einstein-Chern-Simons gravity. The compactification of the extra dimension generates an effective cosmological constant $Λ$ depending on the compactification radius $r_c$, the coupling parameter $l$, and the trace $\tilde{h}$ of the compactified field $h^a$, rather than being introduced as a free parameter. The resulting field equations are structurally equivalent to those of General Relativity with a cosmological constant, so all known vacuum solutions -- Schwarzschild--de Sitter, Kerr--de Sitter, and FLRW spacetimes -- remain valid. As a concrete application, we derive the Kottler (Schwarzschild--de Sitter) black hole solution.
  We identify two dynamical regimes. In the weak-field regime, $Λ\propto l^{2}\tilde{h}/r_{c}^{3}$, whose sign is controlled by $l^2\tilde{h}$, requiring fine-tuning to reproduce $Λ_{\text{obs}} \approx 10^{-52}\,\text{m}^{-2}$. In the strong-field regime, dependence on $l$ and $\tilde{h}$ cancels algebraically, yielding $Λ\approx 3/(4r_{c}^{2})$ independently of the Chern-Simons coupling. This regime naturally reproduces $Λ_{\rm obs}$ for $r_{c} \approx 0.78\,H_{0}^{-1} \approx 8.2 \times 10^{25}\,\text{m}$, without fine-tuning. The Bekenstein-Hawking entropy of the cosmological horizon gives $S_{\rm cosm} = 4πk_B r_c^2/l_{\rm Pl}^2 \sim 10^{122}\,k_B$, consistent with the Gibbons-Hawking result and admitting a direct geometric interpretation in terms of $r_c$. This framework geometrically reframes the cosmological constant problem: rather than asking why $Λ$ is small, one asks why $r_c$ is large -- a reformulation compatible with a large extra dimension without violating established gravitational tests.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a model where the cosmological constant emerges from the compactification of five-dimensional Einstein-Chern-Simons gravity into four dimensions, explaining its small observed value through geometric parameters like the compactification radius, avoiding fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: The cosmological constant problem, specifically why its observed value is extremely small despite theoretical expectations, motivates this geometric approach to inherently derive Λ from compactification without introducing it as a free parameter.
AI.method: The method involves compactifying the fifth dimension in 5D Einstein-Chern-Simons gravity, showing how Λ arises from the compactification radius r_c, coupling parameter l, and trace ħ of the compactified field. Field equations mirror GR with Λ, retaining known solutions like Schwarzschild-de Sitter and FLRW.
AI.result: Two dynamic regimes are identified: a weak-field regime requiring fine-tuning to match Λ_obs, and a strong-field regime where Λ ≈ 3/(4r_c²) naturally matches Λ_obs with r_c ~ 8.2e25 m. The Bekenstein-Hawking entropy for the cosmological horizon aligns with expectations, providing a geometric interpretation.
AI.conclusion: The model reframes the cosmological constant problem by attributing the observed Λ to the size of the compactified dimension rather than its inherent value, addressing fine-tuning issues and integrating with existing gravitational tests via large extra dimension compatibility.

title: The impact of the eROSITA bubbles on Galactic cosmic-ray transport
summary: We propose that the observed spectral hardening in Galactic cosmic ray fluxes is governed by macroscopic Galactic outflows, such as the eROSITA bubbles, rather than microphysical variations in their scattering properties. Employing a phenomenological transport model, we show that an advective outflow boundary naturally reproduces the $300\,$GV hardening in secondary-to-primary ratios. Global fits to precision AMS-02 data yield an effective local inner halo boundary of $\sim 5\,$kpc and an outflow speed of $\sim 360\,$km/s, in striking agreement with independent multi-wavelength kinematic constraints of the eROSITA outflows. This interpretation provides a testable alternative to breaks in the effective diffusion coefficient, without increasing the number of free parameters.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that the observed spectral hardening in Galactic cosmic rays is caused by macroscopic Galactic outflows (e.g., eROSITA bubbles) rather than microphysical scattering variations. A transport model with an advective outflow boundary reproduces the 300 GV hardening in secondary-to-primary ratios, aligning with AMS-02 data and eROSITA outflow constraints.
AI.motivation: The spectral hardening in cosmic ray fluxes has been traditionally attributed to changes in scattering coefficients. The authors seek an alternative explanation using macroscopic structures like outflows to avoid overparameterized models.
AI.method: Phenomenological transport model simulating advective outflow boundaries. Fitted to AMS-02 cosmic ray data to determine halo boundary (~5 kpc) and outflow speed (~360 km/s) parameters.
AI.result: Model successfully reproduces 300 GV hardness energy scale with parameters matching independent eROSITA outflow measurements. Achieves comparable fits to diffusion break models without extra parameters.
AI.conclusion: Galactic outflows provide a viable and testable mechanism for cosmic ray spectral hardening, offering a simpler alternative to microphysical diffusion coefficient modifications.

title: Lights, Camera, Axion: Tracing Axions from Supernovae in the Diffuse $γ$-ray Sky
comment: v1: 25 pages, 10 figures, 1 table. Comments are welcome. For a short video explaining the paper, please see: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Fe21R1D9jYI&t=125s
summary: Axions produced copiously in core-collapse supernovae can convert into photons as they propagate through various astrophysical magnetic fields. The cumulative emission from the cosmic population of supernovae can therefore generate a diffuse gamma-ray signal through axion-photon conversion. In this work, we develop a comprehensive framework to compute the diffuse gamma-ray flux by modeling axion production in supernovae and, \textit{for the first time}, consistently accounting for their conversion into photons across all relevant magnetic field environments - progenitor, host galaxy, intergalactic medium, and the Milky Way - together with an updated cosmic star formation rate. Using measurements of the diffuse gamma-ray sky from COMPTEL, EGRET, and \textit{Fermi}-LAT, we derive competitive constraints on the axion-photon coupling over a wide range of axion masses. We further forecast the sensitivity of upcoming MeV gamma-ray telescopes to this diffuse signal using a Fisher forecast analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how axions from core-collapse supernovae convert into photons across various magnetic fields, calculates the resulting diffuse gamma-ray flux using updated models and data, and derives constraints on axion-photon coupling along with future telescope forecasts.
AI.motivation: To understand the potential gamma-ray signal from axion-photon conversion in cosmic supernova populations and constrain axion properties using existing and future observational data.
AI.method: Developed a framework modeling axion production in supernovae and their photon conversion through multiple magnetic field environments (progenitor stars, galaxies, intergalactic medium, Milky Way), incorporated updated cosmic star formation rates, and analyzed data from COMPTEL, EGRET, Fermi-LAT. Used Fisher forecasts for future telescope sensitivity predictions.
AI.result: Derived competitive constraints on axion-photon coupling across a broad mass range using existing gamma-ray measurements, and showed improved future constraints with upcoming MeV telescopes.
AI.conclusion: The diffuse gamma-ray signal from axion conversion provides a powerful probe of axion physics. Future MeV telescopes could significantly enhance our understanding of axion properties beyond current observational limits.

title: Nonperturbative suppression of beyond-General-Relativity effects in quadratic gravity
summary: Quadratic gravity is a well-motivated extension of general relativity~(GR) wherein the Einstein-Hilbert action is augmented by quadratic curvature terms. This theory is equivalent to GR in an effective-field-theory framework, while the two theories are different at the non-perturbative level. As we have recently shown, black holes in quadratic gravity have a rich linear response, including extra scalar, vector, and tensor quasinormal modes that can be excited in physical processes, even when the stationary solution is the same as in GR. Here, by studying the gravitational-wave emission from point particles plunging into a Schwarzschild black hole in quadratic gravity, we show that observable deviations from GR are exponentially suppressed in the GR limit. This provides a nonperturbative realization of the equivalence between quadratic gravity and GR predicted in the effective-field-theory framework.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that in quadratic gravity, gravitational-wave deviations from GR caused by point particles plunging into Schwarzschild black holes are exponentially suppressed, aligning with the EFT predictived GR-equivalence at the perturbative level despite non-perturbative differences.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether observable differences between quadratic gravity and GR exist in non-perturbative scenarios, specifically examining gravitational-wave emissions from orbiting particles around black holes.
AI.method: Computed gravitational-wave emission from particles plunging into Schwarzschild black holes in quadratic gravity, comparing the results with GR predictions. Analyzed the response of the black hole considering extra quasinormal modes specific to quadratic gravity.
AI.result: Found that deviations in gravitational-wave signals from GR are exponentially suppressed in the GR limit, even when non-perturbative effects like additional quasinormal modes are present.
AI.conclusion: Confirm that quadratic gravity matches GR observations at observable levels in this setup, validating the EFT framework's equivalence prediction despite inherent non-perturbative distinctions between the theories.

title: The Real and Pseudo Dispersion Measures of FRB~20220912A
summary: Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are millisecond-duration radio transients. As they propagate through the interstellar medium, they interact with free electrons, resulting in dispersion. The corresponding dispersion measure (DM) is referred to as the real DM (DM$_{\rm real}$). In practice, however, the dispersion measure derived from modeling (DM$_{\rm model}$) is often contaminated by intrinsic burst morphology, giving rise to a pseudo DM component (DM$_{\rm pseudo} = {\rm DM}_{\rm model} - {\rm DM}_{\rm real}$). In this work, we focus on the highly active repeating FRB~20220912A and utilize its microshots -- extremely short-duration (typically tens of microseconds), broadband emissions -- to investigate its DM$_{\rm real}$ and DM$_{\rm pseudo}$. We adopt two assumptions: first, that FRB~20220912A resides in a non-magneto-ionic environment and that its DM$_{\rm real}$ variation is smaller than $10^{-2}$\,pc\,cm$^{-3}$ over a few years; and second, that microshots have a negligible intrinsic morphological time delay. By identifying two new microshots and combining them with previously reported ones, we find that all four microshots exhibit remarkably consistent DM values over a one-month timescale, with an average of $219.380 \pm 0.004\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$. We define this value as the DM$_{\rm real}$ of FRB~20220912A. We further show that bright, narrow bursts with a width of less than 2\,ms also yield DM estimates consistent with the microshot-based DM$_{\rm real}$. A survey of five repeating FRBs reveals that DM$_{\rm pseudo}$ is a common phenomenon, with variations typically spanning a range of approximately $10\,\mathrm{pc\,cm^{-3}}$ at 1.2\,GHz. These findings highlight the importance of accounting for morphological contributions in DM interpretation and demonstrate that microshots and narrow bursts are powerful tools for probing DM$_{\rm real}$.

AI.tldr: The study analyzes FRB 20220912A's real and pseudo dispersion measures using microshots, finding consistent DM_real and identifying DM_pseudo's presence in multiple FRBs.
AI.motivation: To address inaccuracies in modeled dispersion measures caused by burst morphology and establish reliable methods for determining DM_real.
AI.method: Analyzed microshots (ultrashort emissions) of FRB 20220912A under two assumptions: static real DM and negligible intrinsic time delay in microshots. Combined new and existing data to calculate DM_real and compare with other burst types.
AI.result: Four microshots showed consistent DM_real (~219.38 pc/cm³) over a month. Bright narrow bursts also aligned with this DM_real. Survey of five FRBs confirmed DM_pseudo普遍存在 with variations up to ~10 pc/cm³.
AI.conclusion: Morphological effects significantly impact DM models; microshots and narrow bursts provide precise DM_real measurements, critical for accurate interpretation of FRB dispersion data.

title: Influence of tides and self-gravity on Ultra-Light Dark Matter Bounds from Dwarf Galaxies
summary: Dwarf spheroidal galaxies provide some of the most sensitive astrophysical probes of ultra-light dark matter (ULDM), but the inferred constraints can be affected by two important systematics: tidal interactions with the Milky Way, which reduce ULDM-induced dynamical heating, and stellar self-gravity, which can become relevant if the stellar component was more compact at earlier times. In this work, we attempt to estimate both effects by reconstructing dwarf-galaxy orbital histories in a Milky-Way potential, adopting a simple and approximate tidal-susceptibility diagnostic that we argue provides a conservative description of tidal stripping, and explicitly including stellar self-gravity in our numerical simulations. Within our framework, which we apply to five different dwarf galaxies, we find that ULDM with masses $5\times 10^{-22} \lesssim m/{\rm eV} \lesssim 5\times 10^{-21}$ remains in tension with current data.

AI.tldr: The study examines the impact of tidal interactions and stellar self-gravity on ultra-light dark matter (ULDM) constraints using dwarf spheroidal galaxies. By simulating orbital histories and incorporating these factors, the research finds that ULDM with masses between 5×10⁻²² eV and 5×10⁻²¹ eV conflict with observational data.
AI.motivation: To assess how tidal interactions with the Milky Way and historical stellar self-gravity affect ULDM constraints derived from dwarf galaxies, addressing systematic uncertainties in prior studies.
AI.method: Reconstructed orbital histories of five dwarf galaxies in a Milky Way potential, applied a tidal susceptibility diagnostic, and included stellar self-gravity in simulations to model ULDM dynamics.
AI.result: ULDM masses between 5×10⁻²² eV and 5×10⁻²¹ eV are inconsistent with current observational data when accounting for tidal effects and stellar self-gravity.
AI.conclusion: The analysis reinforces ULDM mass constraints by accounting for key astrophysical systematics, narrowing the viable ULDM parameter range and highlighting the importance of considering tidal stripping and stellar distribution evolution.

title: Radial fall: the gravitational waveform up to the second-and-half Post-Newtonian order
summary: We consider an application of the Multipolar Post Minkowskian formalism to the case of a two-body system in radial fall. We compute, within the post-Newtonian approximation, the associated gravitational waveform reaching the 2.5 Post-Newtonian accuracy level. At this level the presence of a radiation-reaction force manifests, modifying the fall with a corresponding bremsstrahlung radiation. We evaluate then all emissions: energy, angular momentum (vanishing identically) and linear momentum. We also evaluate the (nonlocal) inertial forces contributions appearing (at the next PN order, 4.5PN) in the center-of-mass due to the losses paving the way for future more accurate computations.

AI.tldr: The paper applies the Multipolar Post Minkowskian formalism to a two-body radial infall system, computing gravitational waveforms up to 2.5PN. It analyzes radiation-reaction effects, evaluates energy/angular momentum emissions, and discusses nonlocal inertial forces at 4.5PN for future accuracy improvements.
AI.motivation: To study gravitational radiation and radiation-reaction effects in extreme compact binary systems during radial infall, extending post-Newtonian approximations for precision in waveform modeling and understanding relativistic dynamics.
AI.method: Using Multipolar Post-Minkowskian (MPM) formalism with post-Newtonian expansion, the authors calculate gravitational wave emissions, radiation-reaction forces, and associated bremsstrahlung radiation. Computations include energy, angular momentum, and linear momentum fluxes, with nonlocal inertial force contributions at higher PN orders.
AI.result: Computed gravitational waveforms up to 2.5PN accuracy, confirmed vanishing angular momentum emission, identified radiation-reaction effects influencing the infall dynamics, and evaluated nonlocal contributions at 4.5PN for future reference.
AI.conclusion: The analysis provides essential foundations for high-precision gravitational wave modeling in radially falling binaries, highlighting the necessity of higher-order PN terms and nonlocal effects for advanced computational frameworks in relativistic astrophysics.

title: Gaussian Process Inference of Stochastic Magneto-Active Dynamics and Viscosity in Swift J1727.8-1613
summary: Linking X-ray variability to the underlying magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) dynamics of black hole X-ray binaries remains challenging. We systematically investigate the stochastic and oscillatory variability of the black hole X-ray binary candidate Swift J1727.8$-$1613 during its 2023 outburst using Gaussian process (GP) regression applied to Insight-HXMT multi-band light curves. The variability is modeled with a physically motivated composite kernel comprising one stochastically driven damped simple harmonic oscillator (SHO) and two damped random walk (DRW) components. The SHO term robustly recovers quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs) with frequencies $ν_0 \sim 0.07$--$5$ Hz, consistent with the fundamental Alfvén mode of a contracting magnetically confined disk--coronal cavity. The quality factor rises from $Q \sim 3$ to $Q \sim 10$, suggesting increasing coherence of the magnetic cavity. We also find an anti-correlation between QPO frequency and the short DRW damping timescale, supporting our proposed stochastic magneto-active dynamics scenario. Associating the short and long DRW timescales with the local turbulent turnover and thermal adjustment timescales, respectively, we infer an effective viscosity parameter of $α\approx 0.1$, supporting a strongly magnetized accretion flow. Strikingly, near the onset of relativistic jet ejection around MJD 60206, both relaxation timescales collapse toward the 0.1 s sampling limit, suggesting a rapid reorganization of the disk internal energy balance immediately before jet launching. Our results establish GP inference as a powerful route to connecting X-ray timing observables with the dynamical state of black hole accretion flows.

AI.tldr: The study uses Gaussian process regression to analyze X-ray variability in Swift J1727.8-1613 during its 2023 outburst, linking observations to magnetohydrodynamic dynamics. Key findings include QPOs matching Alfvén mode predictions, an anti-correlation between QPO frequency and DRW damping timescales, and rapid timescale collapse before jet ejection, supporting a strongly magnetized accretion flow model.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between observed X-ray variability and underlying MHD dynamics in black hole X-ray binaries, particularly understanding how stochastic and oscillatory behaviors reflect accretion flow structure and jet formation.
AI.method: Analysis of Insight-HXMT multi-band light curves using Gaussian process regression with a composite kernel (SHO + two DRW components) to model variability. Parameters like QPO frequency, quality factor, and DRW timescales are extracted and physically interpreted.
AI.result: Found QPOs (0.07-5 Hz) consistent with Alfvén mode, increasing Q factor indicating magnetic cavity coherence, anti-correlation between QPO frequency and short DRW timescale, α-viscosity ~0.1, and abrupt timescale collapse prior to jet ejection.
AI.conclusion: GP regression effectively connects timing observables to accretion flow dynamics, supporting a magnetically dominated accretion scenario where jet-launching coincides with rapid energy reorganization marked by shortened relaxation timescales.

title: Reconciling hadronic and partonic analyticity in $b\to s\ell\ell$ transitions
summary: Rare $B$-meson decays mediated by $b\to s\ell\ell$ transitions constitute sensitive probes of physics beyond the Standard Model, and have triggered considerable interest due to hints for deviations from the Standard-Model prediction. To establish a discrepancy beyond a reasonable doubt, control over the nonlocal matrix elements involving charm loops is essential, which, for large spacelike virtualities, can be constrained by an operator product expansion with coefficients known at two-loop order. We observe that the analytic structure of this partonic calculation, whose understanding is important to put forward rigorous parameterizations, follows from simple triangle topologies and demonstrate explicitly how dispersion relations are fulfilled even in the case of anomalous thresholds. Crucially, these anomalous contributions match onto the ones expected when considering hadronic degrees of freedom, proving that the partonic calculation does not miss anomalous effects and justifying its use in regions of parameter space in which a perturbative description applies.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the analysis of rare $B$-meson decays via $b\to s\ell\ell$ transitions, which are key probes for physics beyond the Standard Model (BSM). It focuses on improving control over nonlocal matrix elements with charm loops, particularly in high-virtuality regions, by refining the operator product expansion (OPE) with two-loop coefficients. The authors clarify the analytic structure of the partonic calculation through triangle topology analysis and verify dispersion relations even at anomalous thresholds. They confirm consistency between partonic and hadronic treatments of anomalous effects, validating the OPE's applicability in perturbative regimes.
AI.motivation: To rigorously assess deviations in $b\to s\ell\ell$ transitions from Standard Model predictions, which have hinted at BSM physics. This requires precise control over nonlocal matrix elements involving charm loops, whose behaviors at large spacelike virtualities remain uncertain. Ensuring the validity of the OPE framework—critical for parameterizing these effects—is essential to strengthen conclusions about potential BSM signals.
AI.method: The authors examine the analytic structure of the partonic OPE calculation, using triangle diagram topologies to derive dispersion relations. They explicitly demonstrate adherence to these relations even at anomalous thresholds (kinks in phase-space). The analysis connects partonic results to hadronic expectations, ensuring no anomalous effects are missed in the OPE framework.
AI.result: The study proves that the partonic OPE correctly includes anomalous threshold effects without missing hadronic contributions. This validates its use in parameter regions where perturbation theory applies, providing a solid foundation for future precision tests of BSM physics in $B$-meson decay analyses.
AI.conclusion: The partonic calculation via OPE with two-loop coefficients is justified as a reliable tool for studying $b\to s\ell\ell$ transitions, ensuring precise modeling of charm-loop effects. This work removes concerns about missing anomalous contributions in theoretical frameworks, enabling more robust comparisons between experimental data and Standard Model predictions to identify BSM physics.

title: Interior geometry of black holes as a probe of first-order phase transition
summary: Traditional diagnostics of black hole phase transitions rely on thermodynamic quantities defined at the event horizon or asymptotic boundary. Here, we demonstrate that the near-singularity geometry offers a sharp, independent probe of both first-order phase transitions and supercritical crossover. For scalarized AdS black holes exhibiting a first-order phase transition, the Kasner exponent $p_t$, which characterizes the approach to the singularity, undergoes a dramatic transformation. On one side of the transition, $p_t$ oscillates strongly with temperature, reflecting violent interior dynamics. On the other side, it becomes a smooth, monotonically varying function. These two distinct behaviors converge as the critical point is approached. Beyond the critical point, in the supercritical region, $p_t(T)$ develops a distinct extremum, defining a ''Kasner crossover line'' that is entirely independent of traditional thermodynamic (Widom line) or dynamic (Frenkel line) criteria. Our work establishes the black hole singularity as a novel class of diagnostics for phase transitions, revealing that a change in the macroscopic thermodynamic state fundamentally reshapes the deepest interior structure of spacetime.

AI.tldr: The study introduces the near-singularity geometry of scalarized AdS black holes as a new diagnostic tool for phase transitions. The Kasner exponent $p_t$ shows distinct behaviors across phase boundaries and exhibits a Kasner crossover line beyond the critical point, independent of traditional thermodynamic criteria.
AI.motivation: To explore an alternative method beyond traditional event horizon or boundary-based diagnostics for black hole phase transitions, using the near-singularity region as an independent probe.
AI.method: Analyzing the Kasner exponent $p_t$ in the near-singularity geometry of scalarized AdS black holes, observing its temperature dependence across phase transitions and critical points.
AI.result: The Kasner exponent $p_t$ displays oscillatory behavior on one side of first-order transitions and smooth monotonic behavior on the other, converging near the critical point. Beyond it, a distinct Kasner crossover line emerges independent of Widom/Frenkel lines.
AI.conclusion: Black hole singularities serve as novel diagnostics for phase transitions, showing that macroscopic thermodynamic changes induce fundamental structural alterations in spacetime's deepest interior.

title: GECAM discovery of a peculiar magnetar X-ray burst (MXB 221120) from SGR J1935+2154 associated with a fast radio burst
summary: Fast radio bursts (FRBs) are enigmatic cosmic transients of millisecond duration observed in the radio band. The identification of FRB-associated magnetar X-ray bursts (MXBs) from galactic magnetar SGR J1935+2154 suggests that at least a fraction of FRBs can be produced from magnetar activity. However, the sample size of FRB-associated MXBs is still very small. Here we report a bright and peculiar FRB-associated MXB from SGR J1935+2154 detected by GECAM on November 20, 2022, dubbed MXB 221120. We find that both temporal and spectral properties of MXB 221120 exhibit distinctive features. Its light curve could be generally described by a single FRED function with superposition of several narrow pulses. Interestingly, we identify a possible QPO feature with center frequency of ~18 Hz in this MXB. The time-integrated spectrum is best fitted by a blackbody model with temperature (kT ) of 18.6 keV, rendering it the first thermal spectrum FRB-associated MXB from SGR J1935+2154. Compared to other MXBs with single emission episode, MXB 221120 has longer duration and higher blackbody temperature, making it an outlier in the burst sample. These results indicate that MXB 221120 may be produced by a special mechanism with extreme physical conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper reports a unique FRB-associated magnetar X-ray burst (MXB 221120) from SGR J1935+2154, showing unusual temporal features (FRED profile with superposed pulses), a possible 18 Hz QPO, and a high blackbody temperature (~18.6 keV), suggesting extreme physical conditions and a potential new mechanism for FRBs.
AI.motivation: To expand the small sample of FRB-associated MXBs and investigate their properties to better understand FRB origins, particularly those linked to magnetar activity.
AI.method: Observed MXB 221120 with GECAM satellite; analyzed light curve (FRED function with pulses), searched for QPO features, and fitted spectral data with blackbody models.
AI.result: First thermally fitted FRB-associated MXB from SGR J1935+2154 with highest blackbody temp (~18.6 keV), distinctive light curve profile, and possible 18 Hz QPO. It stands out due to longer duration and higher temperature than known MXBs.
AI.conclusion: The unusual properties suggest MXB 221120 may involve extreme physical conditions or a different mechanism, highlighting diversity in FRB/magnetar爆发 mechanisms and the need for further observations.

title: Q-balls across dimensions
summary: Scalars carrying a conserved global charge $Q$ can form stable localized field configurations composed of a large number of particles. These non-topological solitons are spherically symmetric and are called Q-balls. While usually analyzed in three spatial dimensions, these solitons can be straightforwardly generalized to $d$ spatial dimensions. For $d=1$, we can analytically solve the non-linear differential equation for an important class of single-field potentials; for $d>1$, we can analytically approximate the solutions in the thin-wall or large Q-ball regime, including the first sub-leading correction consistently. Since the underlying differential equations have the same form as vacuum-decay bounce solutions, our results find applications there, too.

AI.tldr: The paper examines Q-ball solutions in various spatial dimensions, providing analytical methods for solving their non-linear differential equations, with applications to vacuum-decay scenarios.
AI.motivation: To extend the analysis of Q-balls beyond three dimensions and develop analytical solutions for their field configurations, especially in the thin-wall regime, to better understand their properties and applicability in different contexts like vacuum decay.
AI.method: The authors solve the non-linear differential equations for Q-balls analytically in 1D and use thin-wall approximations for higher dimensions (d>1), incorporating first sub-leading corrections. They also draw parallels to vacuum-decay bounce solutions.
AI.result: Analytical solutions for Q-balls in 1D are obtained, and approximate solutions with corrections for higher dimensions are derived. These methods are applicable to vacuum-decay processes as well.
AI.conclusion: The derived solutions offer a deeper understanding of Q-ball dynamics across dimensions and their utility in studying phenomena like vacuum decay, highlighting the versatility of the analytical techniques employed.

title: Mitigation of Incoherent Spectral Lines via Adaptive Coherence Analysis for Continuous Gravitational-Wave Searches
summary: The sensitivity of continuous gravitational-wave searches is strictly limited by non-Gaussian spectral artefacts that accumulate coherent power over long observation baselines. In this paper, we present an unsupervised mitigation framework based on adaptive network coherence analysis. Unlike traditional veto methods that discard entire frequency bands, our pipeline selectively suppresses local artefacts while preserving global potentially astrophysical signals. We validate the method using Advanced LIGO O3 data, analysing the cleaning performance across integration times of 1, 3, and 5 days. For the 5-day dataset, the pipeline identifies and mitigates 89\% and 77\% of the total spectral lines in the Hanford and Livingston detectors, respectively, while effectively preserving the coherent population consistent with astrophysical morphologies. This is achieved while modifying less than 7\% of the analysis bandwidth spanning 20~Hz to 2000~Hz. Rigorous statistical verification demonstrates that the mitigation effectively suppresses the non-Gaussian tail of the noise distribution while strictly preserving the statistical integrity of coherent signal candidates. By recovering detector sensitivity in parameter spaces previously contaminated by the spectral forest, this framework provides a robust preprocessing strategy for all-sky searches.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an unsupervised framework to mitigate non-Gaussian spectral artefacts in gravitational-wave data, improving search sensitivity by selectively suppressing artefacts while preserving potential signals. Tested on LIGO O3 data, it reduced artefacts by 77-89% with minimal bandwidth impact.
AI.motivation: To overcome the sensitivity limitations caused by non-Gaussian spectral artefacts in continuous gravitational-wave searches which accumulate coherent power over long observation times.
AI.method: An unsupervised adaptive network coherence analysis method that selectively suppresses local artefacts instead of vetoing entire frequency bands, validated using Advanced LIGO O3 data across 1, 3, and 5-day integrations.
AI.result: For 5-day datasets, the method identified and mitigated 77-89% of spectral lines in both detectors, preserving astrophysical signals while affecting <7% of the 20-2000Hz bandwidth. It suppressed non-Gaussian noise tails without compromising signal candidate statistics.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully recovers detector sensitivity in previously contaminated parameter spaces, offering a robust preprocessing strategy for all-sky gravitational-wave searches.

title: Magneto-Active Environments in Pulsar Binaries with the MeerKAT Telescope: I. Pulsar sample and their basic properties
summary: Eclipsing pulsar binaries and binaries with a high mass companion are ideal systems for studying and understanding the properties of plasma in magneto-ionic environments. In this work, the first paper of a series, we present MeerKAT observations of three pulsar binaries: the high-mass binary PSR J1740$-$3052, the black widow PSR J2051$-$0827 and the redback PSR J1748$-$2446A (Terzan~5A). With the help of MeerKAT's high-sensitivity polarimetric observations, we characterise the properties of these sources, including the linear/circular polarization, dispersion measure (DM), rotation measure (RM) and scattering time. The two eclipsing millisecond pulsars exhibit strong orbital-phase-dependent propagation effects and we observe $\sim$2 eclipses in these systems during our observations. PSR J1740$-$3052 is a binary system with a 231 d orbital period. The relatively large separation results in a smooth RM variation, enabling us to resolve its variation timescale and constrain the small-scale magnetic structure. A gradual RM variation is observed over $\sim$1500 s, occurring near periastron. This behaviour implies a magnetic spatial scale of $\sim$0.003 AU in the companion wind, assuming a relative velocity of $\sim$250 km s$^{-1}$. The linear polarisation intensity profiles of PSR J2051$-$0827 show shape variations as a function of frequency, with a stronger leading component emerging at lower frequencies. We observe signatures of the propagation effect in the polarisation properties of PSR J1748$-$2446A during eclipse ingress and egress. This could arise from Faraday Conversion or multipath propagation of the pulsar signal and requires detailed analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses MeerKAT observations of three pulsar binaries to study plasma properties in their environments, focusing on polarization effects and orbital phase dependencies.
AI.motivation: To characterize plasma properties in magneto-ionic environments using pulsar binaries, particularly eclipsing and high-mass systems, through their polarization and propagation effects.
AI.method: High-sensitivity polarimetric observations with MeerKAT were used to measure parameters like linear/circular polarization, DM, RM, and scattering time. Analysis focused on orbital-phase-dependent variations in RM and polarization profiles across frequencies.
AI.result: Observed phase-dependent RM variations in eclipsing pulsars, identified a 0.003 AU magnetic structure in PSR J1740-3052's companion wind, noted frequency-dependent polarization changes in PSR J2051-0827, and detected eclipse-related polarization anomalies in PSR J1748-2446A.
AI.conclusion: MeerKAT observations reveal detailed plasma and magnetic field structures in pulsar binaries. Results suggest Faraday Conversion or multipath propagation mechanisms, emphasizing the need for further detailed analysis to fully interpret observed effects.

title: High-Frequency Gravitational Wave Constraints from Graviton-Photon Conversion in the M87 Galaxy
summary: High-frequency gravitational waves, particularly in the range $f \gtrsim 10^{10}~\mathrm{Hz}$, represent a compelling probe of physics beyond the Standard Model. Due to the absence of direct detection methods in this frequency regime, alternative strategies may be pursued. One promising approach involves the conversion of gravitons into photons in the presence of magnetic fields, a process known as the inverse Gertsenshtein effect. In this study, we explore such graviton-to-photon conversions occurring within the magnetic field environment of the M87 galaxy, utilizing realistic models for the galactic magnetic field and plasma density structure. We use the broadband electromagnetic spectrum of M87, ranging from millimeter to TeV gamma rays, to search for hidden contributions from graviton-photon conversions. In the well-constrained frequency range $10^{10}$-$10^{27}~\mathrm{Hz}$, the lack of excess emission allows us to place improved bounds on the gravitational wave strain amplitude $h_c$ or on spectral energy density $Ω_{\mathrm{gw}} h^2$. We find that our results from M87 yield substantially stronger constraints compared to existing bounds derived from Milky Way magnetic field considerations, with improvements ranging from one to five orders of magnitude depending on the frequency band, thereby enhancing the prospects for probing high-frequency gravitational wave backgrounds through indirect electromagnetic signatures.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using the inverse Gertsenshtein effect in M87's magnetic field to detect high-frequency gravitational waves (f ≥ 10¹⁰ Hz) via their conversion to photons. By analyzing M87's electromagnetic spectrum, the study sets tighter constraints on gravitational wave strain or energy density than previous Milky Way-based methods, improving bounds by 1-5 orders of magnitude across frequencies.
AI.motivation: High-frequency gravitational waves are inaccessible to direct detection methods, necessitating alternative strategies like photon-graviton conversions in cosmic magnetic fields. M87's strong magnetic environment provides a better laboratory than the Milky Way to probe these waves indirectly through their imprint on electromagnetic observations.
AI.method: The authors model M87's magnetic field and plasma structure to calculate graviton-to-photon conversion efficiencies. They compare predicted excess photon emissions with M87's observed broadband spectrum (millimeter to TeV) to derive constraints on gravitational wave parameters (h_c or Ω_gw h²) across 10¹⁰–10²⁷ Hz.
AI.result: No excess emission detected, leading to improved upper limits on gravitational wave strain/energy density.
